```json
{
  "bottleneck_type": "memory-bound",
  "data_dependencies": [
    "Each element A[i][j] depends on the values of its neighbors from the previous iteration, creating a data dependency across iterations.",
    "The computation of A[i][j] in the current iteration depends on A[i-1][j-1], A[i-1][j], A[i-1][j+1], A[i][j-1], A[i][j], A[i][j+1], A[i+1][j-1], A[i+1][j], and A[i+1][j+1] from the previous iteration."
  ],
  "parallelization_challenges": [
    "The inherent data dependencies across iterations make it difficult to parallelize the time steps directly.",
    "Within a single time step, the computation of A[i][j] depends on neighboring elements, which can lead to race conditions if not handled properly."
  ],
  "recommended_strategy": "Use a double-buffering technique where two arrays are used: one for reading the current state and another for writing the updated state. This allows parallelization within each time step by assigning each thread to compute a specific element A[i][j] independently, while ensuring that reads are from the previous state and writes are to the new state. Utilize shared memory to cache neighboring elements for each thread block to reduce global memory accesses.",
  "gpu_suitable": true
}
```
full.stage2:
```json
{
  "validation": "partial",
  "corrected_analysis": "Bottleneck: likely memory-bound for large n (9 loads + 1 store per point, low arithmetic intensity), though cache reuse can help somewhat. The dependency analysis is incomplete/misleading: this kernel is Gauss–Seidel (in-place) because A[i][j] is overwritten and subsequent points in the same time step read the updated values (not purely 'previous iteration' values). Specifically, within a fixed t, A[i][j] depends on A[i][j-1] and A[i-1][*] that have already been updated in the same sweep order, while it depends on A[i][j+1] and A[i+1][*] that are not yet updated. Therefore, a naive 2D parallel mapping (one thread per (i,j) updating in-place) violates true loop-carried dependencies and changes semantics. Double-buffering would remove these intra-time-step dependencies but would change the algorithm to Jacobi (different numerical method / different results for finite tsteps), so it does not preserve semantics unless you explicitly accept that change. Correct parallelization must respect Gauss–Seidel ordering, e.g., via wavefront (anti-diagonal) scheduling or red-black (multi-color) Gauss–Seidel (still not identical to lexicographic GS unless you accept that semantic change).",
  "gpu_strategy": {
    "approach": "Wavefront (anti-diagonal) Gauss–Seidel with one kernel launch per wavefront (or per tile-wavefront), in-place update",
    "description": "Preserve the exact lexicographic (i outer, j inner) Gauss–Seidel semantics by executing updates in dependency order. For a given time step t, define wavefronts by s = i + j. Because each update depends on (i-1, j-1), (i-1, j), (i-1, j+1), and (i, j-1) which all have smaller (i+j) than (i,j), executing increasing s ensures all required 'already-updated' neighbors are complete. For each s from 2 to 2*(n-2), launch a GPU kernel that updates all (i,j) with i in [1,n-2], j in [1,n-2], and i+j = s. All points on the same anti-diagonal are independent and can run in parallel. This keeps the in-place array A and matches the CPU loop order’s dataflow (up to reordering within the same anti-diagonal, which is safe because there are no dependencies among same-s points). To reduce launch overhead, use tiled wavefront: partition the domain into BxB tiles; process tiles in tile-wavefront order (tile_i + tile_j increasing). Within a tile, compute elements in local wavefront order using shared memory for the tile plus 1-cell halo, writing back to global after finishing the tile. This reduces global traffic and reduces the number of global synchronizations from O(n) wavefronts to O(n/B) tile-wavefronts (still requiring a global barrier between tile-wavefronts, typically implemented as separate kernel launches).",
    "preserves_semantics": true,
    "kernel_design": "Two-level wavefront. (1) Host loop over t. (2) Host loop over tile wavefront index ws = tile_i + tile_j. Launch kernel<<<grid(ws), block>>> where each block handles one tile on that ws. In-kernel: load (B+2)x(B+2) region into shared memory (halo included). Then for local wavefront k=0..2*(B-1): threads whose (li+lj)==k update shared[li+1][lj+1] using the 3x3 stencil from shared; __syncthreads() between k steps. After finishing, store the BxB interior back to A. Boundary tiles handle edges by clamping/conditional loads; global boundaries (i=0,n-1 or j=0,n-1) are not updated per original code."
  },
  "expected_speedup": "Moderate. Compared to a CPU baseline, speedup can be significant for large n, but it will be limited by (a) global synchronization frequency (kernel launches per tile-wavefront per t) and (b) memory bandwidth. Versus an incorrect fully-parallel in-place GPU version, this is slower but correct. Versus a Jacobi (double-buffer) GPU version, this is typically slower due to synchronization, but preserves Gauss–Seidel semantics.",
  "implementation_notes": [
    "Do NOT use simple double-buffering if you must preserve exact Gauss–Seidel semantics; that changes the method to Jacobi.",
    "Naive 2D thread mapping updating A in-place is incorrect due to loop-carried dependencies (not just race conditions).",
    "Tile size B: typically 16 or 32 for doubles depending on shared memory limits: shared bytes ~ (B+2)^2 * 8. Example B=32 => ~9 KB, fine; but occupancy also depends on registers.",
    "Within-tile wavefront requires __syncthreads() per local diagonal; cost is acceptable because it is intra-block.",
    "Global ordering between tiles must follow tile-wavefronts; easiest correct mechanism is one kernel launch per tile-wavefront (global barrier). For large tsteps, consider CUDA Graphs to reduce launch overhead.",
    "Memory access: load/store coalescing is best when threads map to contiguous j. When loading halos, use cooperative loads with linear thread indexing.",
    "Use restrict-qualified pointers and consider __ldg (or rely on modern caching) for read-only loads; but note reads are from A that is being updated, so only safe within the staged shared-memory tile.",
    "If you can relax semantics (accept different iteration ordering), red-black Gauss–Seidel (2-color) gives much more parallelism (2 kernels per t) but is not identical to lexicographic GS; only use if acceptable."
  ]
}
```
